column7: They could understand the severity of pain endometriosis can cause in the body & how the body can be affected. When they are not properly educated on endo, an example one dr told me a hysterectomy will cure it & I don't have Adenomyosis, they can't possible understand the severity of pain. I had it removed from my ureters, bladder flaps, all over my peritoneum, discoid bowel resection, appendectomy & a presacral neurectomy performed. If doctors understand current facts about endo then they could actually understand the pain & not suggest Tylenol or hysterectomy. I did my own research to find an expert to operate, none of my doctors even knew they existed.
column10: It starts as a constant dull achy full feeling in my pelvis & can advance to stabbing knives which causes me to vomit & sometimes pass out
column11: Yes the ACOG & other country's guiding bodies for GYN could listen to the endo community & educate current providers as well as properly educate current medical students. I have heard medical students get 1 slide about endometriosis; a disease that affects 1 in 10 women, let that sink in for a minute. We desperately need all medical professionals to be educated about endometriosis using current evidenced based data. Dr. Redwine is a pioneer & has a ton of data to back up what we (the patients) are trying to explain. Please help educate them so more women can get excision surgery sooner & not suffer for many years in pain like me.

column7: Guiding the patient through questions that are determined by a endometriosis specialist (a true one).  Who is aware of physical and mental symptoms.
column10: It feels like all over body pain when inflamed.  But my endometriosis had infiltrated my nerves in pelvis. Stage 4 (complicated case) I feel like the all over pain is nerve related.  The abdominal cramps, gallbladder symptoms,  breathing issues were all the symptoms from my endometriosis.  I had surgery in Dec 2017 and it is getting better daily.  I have a higher endurance and am able to be present and not consumed with my disease pain.  I DO have pain after surgery but it is a healing pain not disease pain (I can feel the difference).  Mindfulness classes and pelvic floor therapy and diet changes are my primary focuses in getting the most out of my excision surgery.  I am so thankful to have had a doctor so close to me.
column11: Recognition of facts and proven studies and NOT utilizing unproven treatment methods BEFORE determining stage and severity of endo through examination of pelvic floor muscles sensitivity, transvaginal ultra sound , mental health evaluation.  And on top of that some sort of material on self care in regards to diet.  Referal for food allergy test.  After all these evaluations have been completed to rule out any other issues (that would be causing extreme unnatural pain. ) If pain persists then laparoscopy to determine extent of disease (diagnosis purposes only)  ONLY documenting what is seen and not attempting to utilize ablation to burn disease which in fact makes the disease harder to see and there for to remove at the time of excision surgery.  Same thing applies with birth control.  Surgeons that are utilizing birth control are seeing less inflammation when they go into an abdomen after being treated with a birth control or Lupron.  This is only proof that the disease is less visible to surgeons who treat patients with birth control or Lupron.

Be open to the suggestion of Endo. Its better to check things and rule things out rather than treat people like they're crazy because you don't believe them or don't want to do the tests to show one way or the other.

column10: Sometimes its shooting pain through the bun as my rectum is attached to my uterus by adhesions. It's a very overwhelming pain that sometimes cant be fixed by pain medication and heat pack and rest. It stops you from doing things, work, friends etc and as it affects my bowels sometimes I just cant leave the house when its bad.

This might not ease their pain, but at least they would feel less alone through their journey with endometriosis.

column7: If you suffer from chronic pain, the 1-10 scale isn‰ŰŞt effective as the median is different. There are also different types of pain like stabbing, sharp, shooting, throbbing, aching etc that impacts the 1-10 scale.
column10: This differs on the time of the month and the day. An example could be day 1 starts with shooting pains in legs and an ache in the lower back before moving to a a deep throbbing pain in the lower stomach which escalates to stabbing pain before a constant long and sharp pain.
column11: Having a different scale of pain than 1-10. Possibly a different chart using different types of pain and their severity - similar to a risk rating table.

column10: Sometimes being kicked or something being forcefully jammed in the vagina.  Cramping front and back.

column7: On my journey to diagnosis it wouldn't be uncommon for me to have "brain fog", finding the right words would be difficult so perhaps if they had a list of differing pain relatable words I could have circled for them that may have helped.
column10: It differs.. Sometimes a stabbing or ripping sensation. More commonly for me its a heavy throbbing ache almost like having an awful bruise that is continually irritated and won't heal. At times the ache is sharper, i could compare it to the pain associated with toothache but throughout my abdomen.
column11: Being listened to and able to find the right words would help.

column7: Have different words to help me describe the nature of it. I tend to pass out when I have the pain, which I think meant doctors were less inclined to dismiss my description than if I'd been describing pain alone
column10: it feels like something is clenching and yanking my whole abdomen, it comes in 10 second waves, and the pain feels deep inside
column11: Build awareness of it more generally - I had heard of it through friends, but not in the media

column2: 20 years since I started having periods, less than a year since I discussed it with my current GP. I had mentioned pain to two doctors in the meantime.
column7: I'm not sure this applies to my case, but I suppose they could ask about the things I mention in item 9.
column10:
It feels like my inner organs are being flayed
I have a stabbing pain in my ovaries
It feels like spasms
I feel weak and faint and with no energy
I'd say it's 8/10
I am unable to work and function normally when I have period pain - it only recedes if I take very strong painkillers and stay warm in bed
I don't feel like eating
I have so much pain I sometimes can't sleep, or I wake up in pain once the painkiller wears off
I still feel pain and need to stay in bed even if I have taken strong prescription painkillers like co-codamol or dicolfenac
I usually need to take 3 days off work and stay in bed every time I have my period
column11:
I really don't think that I am unable to describe my pain. My problem has been that I've always thought that this amount of pain is normal, and nobody told me otherwise. My mother didn't have this so I suppose she assumed I don't have it either. I think the main problem about not diagnosing endometriosis has to do with not enabling women to talk about it at all, rather than not telling them how to talk about it. Some things I think would help:
- opportunity to talk. e.g. being asked at regular intervals by her healthcare professional how her periods feel, just like regular smear tests. other people (e.g. teachers, parents and employers/workplaces, especially colleagues looking into health, wellbeing and equality and inclusivity) should be educated about the condition and be alert to it, exactly the same as we now try to raise awareness and train people about mental health issues and other conditions (e.g. dyslexia).
- a supportive environment in which to talk. we have to stop making women feeling guilty for having periods, pain and/or complaining about it. Make sure we don't trivialise or stigmatise women's concerns, whether about their health or anything else. endo could be the focus of a targeted campaign again much in line with mental health awareness campaigns.
- more accurate and widespread information about periods, including about endometriosis. this means trying to stop the topic being taboo, but also amending any reproductive health information currently available. For a long time I was convinced I don't have it, because I had read that sufferers often vomit or collapse (I didn't). I also didn't know what counts as 'heavy flow' until recently (I have it).
